speaker: interviewer
text: Interview with Lola Snow. It is the 10th of November, 1995. Lola--

speaker: interviewee
text: It's 57 years since a Kristallnacht.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the anniversary of Kristallnacht. When did your life change? Talking of Kristallnacht, what-- what happened to change your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: The death of my father changed my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lola, when did that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: In December, 1937.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a schoolgirl. My father was sick in bed with a coronary. And suddenly, at night, there was a knock at the door. And they said there's police.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't believe. What are police doing with-- so I rang the police. Imagine? I was so young, and I had enough brain to ring the police if it's true that they sent somebody. And they said, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: They came. And they said that they want my father to get out of bed. It was night-- and to come with them. And I-- and we--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why? Why? What was the reason?

speaker: interviewee
text: First-- first-- first we said he can't get up, because he had-- he is a sick person. We can give all the certificates from all the doctors that say-- can't get up. So then they-- it was going on like this. I don't know for how many hours. And then they said, apparently-- I-- I went to school. Because he was staying-- stayed home. And we had all the hope--

speaker: interviewer
text: This was the next morning already?

speaker: interviewee
text: The next morning. We had all the hopes that he will stay home because of the doctor's certificate and everything. And, apparently, they said they need him as a witness for somebody, and it is essential. And it won't interfere with his health. And he will come back soon. And that's how they got him out from home. When I came back from school, I saw my father standing on a train stop with my brother-- I don't remember if my mother too-- going to the next city, to Sosnowiec. And I didn't know what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who else was with him? Was he with policeman, or alone?

speaker: interviewee
text: With police-- I think it was policemen-- as I-- I visualize this. It was policemen.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he had an escort? He Had people--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --around him?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I didn't know what happened. I came home. And I-- I-- they said-- as I saw I-- all my-- my father's relative were in Sosnowiec. And they took him to Sosnowiec there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I-- I-- I-- I-- I don't know how, or who gave me the initiative, or the-- the wisdom to go there. But I did go. And then I saw my-- my father sitting-- not in a jail, but like in a jail room-- and waiting there. Where? Where were--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Sosnowiec.

speaker: interviewer
text: But in a police station?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a police station. It must have been a police. It wasn't jail. It must have been a police station. And-- and I was very worried about him, because I knew-- they say that he had to go to Warsaw to-- to be witness there. And it was a long, long trip to Warsaw. So I decided on my own--

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe 15, 15 and a 1/2-- that I am going back to-- to Bedzin. And I get his tallit, which was the most important thing for him-- to be able to pray-- and also that I get his-- his medication, the insulin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father was diabetic?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a-- a sugar diabetic. I was born, apparently, when he was already a sugar diabetic. And I never got sugar diabetic.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went. And I got those essentials for him, and took a taxi back to Sosnowiec. When I came there, there were so many people there. And they took my father out of this police station. And we were going to the train station. It was like a-- a live funeral. What-- people had just come to watch and see?

speaker: interviewee
text: People just came to say-- to farewell him somehow-- somehow. Everybody was walking after my father. And my brother went together with him in the train to-- to Warsaw. And he thought that in Warsaw he'd be able to have some, you know, some connection to somebody who can-- who can verify that my father hasn't done anything, that he is-- that he is not guilty of nothing. Because then we knew that they-- they will-- to carry-- take him to Kartuz-Bereza.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what Kartuz-Bereza was.

speaker: interviewee
text: Kartuz-Bereza was the first concentration camp in Poland. They used to take the really like communist, and people which were-- they-- they used to-- to treat the people there like in-- in labor camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say, they, you mean the-- who-- who-- who controlled and ran Kartuz-Bereza?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Polish of-- of Polish police.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was a-- a Polish government--

speaker: interviewee
text: A Polish--

speaker: interviewer
text: --or police, camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes-- camp. Yes. And that's why my-- my-- my brother couldn't get an audition to anybody of those big-- big people, like rich [NON-ENGLISH] at that time, or whoever was at the-- at the top of the government. And they promised him that he will be able to talk to them on Monday. But in the meantime, they took my father away into the-- with-- with the police, which were pretty nice to him. My brother said that they were cracking jokes even. And the police told me then-- told us or told me-- that my father put on the tallit when he was close to Bereza. And he was praying. And then he complained that he had got-- got pains in his chest. But they wouldn't believe him.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they took him down from the Kartuz-Bereza train-- in Kartuz-Bereza, from the train, to a buggy and horse. And in that buggy and horse, he was supposed to reach Kartuz-Bereza. But when they got to Kartuz-Bereza, they tried to push him-- to take him down. He-- he was dead already. He never reached Kartuz-Bereza.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [CRYING] He must have been-- I don't know. I think it must have been Friday night when he-- when he died, because I remember my mother was lighting the candle. And one candle blew out. And I got such a shock. I remember this. And then it was Saturday. And the police came.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they say, who is he? They say oh, maybe my mother, an elderly woman already-- because as I told you, I was born when-- when they were nearly 40. So they took-- my brother wasn't there. And they took me to the police. And I didn't know what for.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the meantime, my brother found out what happened. And he came to the police station. And he went in. And I could see-- could hear him crying. So I knew already what happened. And I cried. I cried. They took me again home on a buggy and horse. And I don't think that I-- that I had-- that I had one breathe without crying. I was screaming. But when I got home, I fainted. I remember this clearly. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And that was-- that was hard. It took three or four days that they could-- could get the corpse of my father back from Kartuz-Bereza to Bedzin. I think one uncle from Lwow went. And one uncle from Sosnowiec went. And they brought him back. And on a Tuesday was the funeral.

speaker: interviewer
text: What date--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: --was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Hanukkah-- fifth day of Hanukkah when he died. So I'd celebrate--

speaker: interviewer
text: And the year-- and the year was? 9--

speaker: interviewee
text: '37. And I'd celebrate his [NON-ENGLISH] according to the fifth day of Hanukkah. I just got yesterday a letter from the synagogue that it falls this year, on the 21st of December.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me about the funeral.

speaker: interviewee
text: What can I tell you about the funeral? The-- all shops were closed in the whole city. They were taking my father from every synagogue-- from one synagogue to another synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- the-- the body?

speaker: interviewee
text: The body. The whole school, everybody-- I don't remember anymore anything. But I know that it was a grief for the whole city. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And when the-- I remember when-- when we were sitting Shiva, I said-- I remember the kids were coming and trying to comfort me. They-- they said that next week they are having an essay from-- from Polish, or that we're going for an excursion here and there. And I remember clearly that I said, how can anybody make plans? If anything like this could happen so sudden, you can't plan anymore. So this-- this event, besides the-- the personal tragedy--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --for you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --changed something in you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I needed something-- I needed an ideal. I needed something that I could believe in. And I found a-- a-- a boy who told me how to become a great Zionist.

speaker: interviewer
text: So-- so this profoundly-- you-- you changed. You grew up. You were not a child anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: I grew up. I grew-- anymore. I didn't listen anymore to-- to-- to anybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: From that point on? And you had this very strong feeling that people couldn't make plans, that the future--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --was--

speaker: interviewee
text: The future-- I-- when I-- when I told my mother, you know, slowly and slowly, Vienna was-- Austria was occupied by the German. Sudeten was occupied by the German in 19-- Vienna in-- in-- in March '38; Sudeten in March '30-- in September '38.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said to my mother, why don't you sell everything? And we'll got to Palestine. I said, what for? I've got Palestine here. This is what your mother said? So you became very Zionistic.

speaker: interviewee
text: I became very Zionistic. And I-- I had a-- a boyfriend which was very Zionistic. And we hoped one day to go to Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother--

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was against it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You wouldn't-- wouldn't hear-- wouldn't hear of this.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no. No, my mother was against the boy. My mother was against him. But that boy followed me everywhere I went with my mother. If I went to this place for holiday, or to this place for holiday, he followed me always. And that how he followed me, even when I was running away when the war broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me-- tell me a little bit about the changes that occurred after your-- you mentioned briefly, after your father's death the-- the increasing power of Germany, and that Austria was taken.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Sudeten [NON-ENGLISH] was taken. And we all expected that the next will be Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: You thought that Poland would be next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Will be the next-- definitely. We didn't know when, and didn't know-- we even went in the summer of '39 with my mother to a very beautiful place, which was called [NON-ENGLISH] where my mother was you know, like-- my mother was having spas there and things like the--

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland. My parents were-- went everywhere in-- in-- in Germany and in-- in Czechoslovakia. They never went to America or to-- nobody went that-- they-- so far. But in that summer of 1939, I had a ball there. And my mother wasn't very well. She had some trouble with some gallstones, I remember. And it became so clear that the war is going to break out anyway, that we decided in the last minute to come home. We came home maybe on the 30th of August, on the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Of which year? In '30--

speaker: interviewee
text: In '39. And the first of September, the war broke out. And as we lived so very close to the German border-- it was maybe 16 kilometers to German border from us.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we decided-- my mother had a brother in the center of Poland, a city called Piotrkow, which doesn't mean anythings. And we decided we'd go there. You and your mother were going to pack up and-- and-- and go to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Pack-- we packed up and we went by train.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went to Piotrkow.

speaker: interviewee
text: Piotrkow, yes. And we stayed there. And that boyfriend of mine followed us to there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your boyfriend's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shlomo Schiff.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Shlomo followed you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you remain with your uncle?

speaker: interviewee
text: We remained only a day or two, because we thought it'll take the German most probably a year to conquer Poland. But it took him a day, and he was already nearly in Piotrkow.

speaker: interviewee
text: So me and Shlomo decided we'll go-- we further. And we were-- his family was somewhere not far from the border of-- of the Russia-- far, but much closer than-- than from there, from the Russian border. We said, we're going to-- to meet his parents in Sanok. Sanok was further east.

speaker: interviewee
text: Further east. And we-- we-- from there, we went further. It took us 18 days. We decided we're going to the Russian border.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, where was your mother? Was she--

speaker: interviewee
text: She left. She stayed in Piotrkow.

speaker: interviewer
text: She stayed with her brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, with her-- with her brother and his family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did she feel about you moving on?

speaker: interviewee
text: She wasn't very happy. But I said, I'm afraid. And I'm not staying here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you traveled with-- with Shlomo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his family were there as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: His family too. His family were fantastic to me, really. Till today, his sister writes to me letters. And we-- we went as far as the-- really, the border town of Poland and Russia--

speaker: interviewer
text: This--

speaker: interviewee
text: --which was Ostrog.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it took you, you mentioned, 18 days?

speaker: interviewee
text: 18 Days.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you travel?

speaker: interviewee
text: We traveled by foot. We traveled by buggy and horse. We traveled by car. We traveled by anything that was possible. And we traveled. And the bombs were falling down wherever we were going. People are-- were dying. And-- and-- and blood was spread everywhere where we looked.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were part of this wave of refugees who were just moving east?

speaker: interviewee
text: Moving east. But the big move east was after they occupied the heart of Poland. You know, because it came in like this-- that when we got to Ostrog, which was the border city of Poland and Russia, suddenly we saw the Russian troops coming forward towards us. And we couldn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were coming westwards.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, and we couldn't understand what happened. And then we found out that Hitler and Stalin make the pact that part of Poland will be occupied by the German, and part of Poland will be occupied by the Russian. And as it happened, I was now under-- under the Russian occupation. And my mother was under the German occupation. What to do now?

speaker: interviewer
text: So you-- you were physically separated by two different powers. You were with the Russians. Your mother was under German occupation.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were with the Schiff family.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was with the Schiff family. But I also had-- as I told, you-- remember, that one of my uncles from Lwow went to collect the corpse of my father. So I had an aunt and a-- and-- and-- and her family, my-- my-- my father's sister and her family, who lived in Lwow. So we moved back to Lwow, and I--

speaker: interviewer
text: From Ostrog?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Ostrog. We only stayed there a few-- a few days. And I lived with my-- my aunt.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then you left the Schiffs--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and you moved in with your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --uncle and aunt. And you are now Lwow. And Lwow was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --controlled by?

speaker: interviewee
text: By the-- I even wanted to go to uni in Lwow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was Lwow under-- controlled by at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: By the Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: By the Russians.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I must say that the-- I was under the Russians from September '39 till June '41. And I must say, I was quite lucky there. My brother came there, because he also crossed the border, because everybody wanted so that it's much better to be under the Russian than to be under the German.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your brother came to Lwow with--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, to Lwow.

speaker: interviewer
text: --with his wife and--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. By-- by--

speaker: interviewer
text: Alone?

speaker: interviewee
text: Alone with another cousin. You know, everybody was running.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So your mother was still back in Bedzin now.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bedzin.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you and your--

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister-in-law was in Tomaszow, where she comes from, with-- with my brother's child. And me and my brother were in-- in Lwow. We didn't live together, because he came with another cousin. And he lived with the cousin. I was knitting. And he was selling what I knitted on the market.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you became a knitter?

speaker: interviewee
text: I became a knitter. I became a top knitter. Would you imagine me, that I do-- couldn't do a thing? But I only knew how to knit as a little girl. They created a cooperative under the Russians. And they took me there. And I became the quickest. I was the quickest knitter of 188 girls. And because of this-- in Russia, I don't know if you know what it is-- a [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell-- tell me what is-- tell me what it is.

speaker: interviewee
text: A [NON-ENGLISH] is somebody who excels in anything, who is the quickest in running, the quickest in knitting, the quickest, the best, the best at school, [NON-ENGLISH]. You are the best.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were the champion knitter?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was the champion knitter. And so I had to deliver speeches, which I didn't believe into. But I had to speak the way they want me to speak-- on the first of-- of May, on the eighth of March, which is the Women's Day. And you know, I-- I-- I-- I earned a lot of money that way. And I was able to send my mother parcels. I was all the time in-- in touch with my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you keep this contact with your mother? Was post--

speaker: interviewee
text: Post.

speaker: interviewer
text: By post?

speaker: interviewee
text: By-- by post.

speaker: interviewer
text: Regular post.

speaker: interviewee
text: Regular post, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: That continued?

speaker: interviewee
text: That continued, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you became a great worker.

speaker: interviewee
text: A great worker.

speaker: interviewer
text: A Soviet worker.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I found a-- I-- I had a boyfriend. And I had-- and he was studying medicine. And he continued to study medicine at the Lwow University. And he was-- as it happened, he lived in the same house as my aunt lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: This-- so Shlomo was-- you weren't with Shlomo--

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, finished.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was finished?

speaker: interviewee
text: Finished, finished. I don't want to talk about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. so you had a new boyfriend?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you remained in the knitting factory. How long did you stay as-- knitting? How long did that continue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Till the war broke out--

speaker: interviewer
text: Till--

speaker: interviewee
text: --till the war broke out again between Germany and-- and Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. This-- there's no words that can describe the disaster that happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ugh. There were-- they were knocking on every Jewish door, dragging out the Jews, putting them-- killing them on the street, or putting them in-- in some big orphanages, or some-- then from that, taking them to labor camp, to concentration camp. There were thousands, thousands of them dying every-- every minute and every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say, they, this is now the SS, the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans-- the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you while this was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- I was with my aunt. And we were lucky. They were knocking on our down. And my aunt had a Ukrainian servant. And the Ukrainian servant spoke Ukrainish to them. And they thought that they-- we don't-- nobody lives here-- of-- of Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she say that there were no Jews there?

speaker: interviewee
text: There's no Jews here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: Antonia.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Antonia.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she saved you, the Ukrainian lady, the servant, at that particular point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, at this-- at that particular-- because I got saved hundreds of times. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then we had to move to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was the ghetto in Lwow?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Lwow.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was that move organized? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember how. This is a blank, completely blank. I know that I've been in the ghetto. How I got to the ghetto, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you with your aunt?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was with my aunt, and with my uncle--

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: --and with-- and with-- with her daughter. Her son got taken to the army by the Russian, you know, while we were under the Russian occupation. He was at the right age to go to the army. We never heard from him anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the ghetto in Lwow.

speaker: interviewee
text: The ghetto was terrible. We didn't have food to eat. We didn't have-- we had nothing. My-- my uncle was quite well off. And he had some money-- some put away. And he could help a little bit of buying this and this. But I decided I go out of the ghetto looking for a-- for a job somewhere. Maybe I can-- I can-- I didn't look Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did you get out of the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got out of the-- I just-- you know, it wasn't yet a ghetto that you-- you-- you had to pass something. I just went out. And I went. And I found a job in a German laundry. I was washing every day the washing that come-- came from the front-- full of lice, full of bacillus, full of dirt. And I was-- there was no washing machine. There was no dryers-- scrubbing like this. And, of course, my hands got swollen. My feet got swollen. And I was-- I was really pretty, pretty bad off. But I was young. You know what it is when you are-- you're one day not well, and then the next day you're well. And my-- and I was able to bring some bread home to my family too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you managed to work outside the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Outside the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: But returned to the ghetto--

speaker: interviewee
text: Returned--

speaker: interviewer
text: --every night?

speaker: interviewee
text: --every night to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- you were wearing the star, yellow star?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember-- no. I most probably-- no, when I was going out of the ghetto, I wasn't wearing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So were you illegally going out of the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Illegally-- illegally going out of the ghetto. I was illegally going to another city to give-- to bring food to the-- to the family of my boyfriend that lived there, to bring them news, even horrible news, that his brother and his sister-in-law got killed by the German. And one day, when I came home from this laundry, there was a man there. And he-- he handed me a little note. And on the note was written, please trust this man. And I recognize my mother's handwriting. And my mother sent him. She paid him most probably lots of money, because he was a smuggler-- to smuggle me back to her, because between me and my mother was a border. Where I-- where I was, it was called the German protectorate. Where my mother was was called the Drittes Reich. And she thought, hearing what atrocities were going on in this part of occupied Poland, by the German, that I have more chance if I be with her. So she sent this man. And I trust him. I left my aunt. And I went with him. Don't ask me how long it took, how dangerous it was, and how many times we were nearly caught. But I reached my mother in December, 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in December 1941, with the help of this smuggler--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --you returned back to Bedzin.

speaker: interviewee
text: I returned back to Bedzin, to my mother. And I met my mother in the street. It was-- it was a fantastic reunion. She somehow grew up too. She wasn't the same as she was. She wanted my love. She didn't want me to be scared of her anymore. She only wanted my love. And my mother lived, at that time already, not in the center of the ghetto. But she was thrown out from our beautiful home. And she was living in somebody's kitchen, in the back of the kitchen, divided by a wardrobe. And there I stayed with my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was in the ghetto? This--

speaker: interviewee
text: In the ghetto-- not quite the ghetto, but outskirts of-- of the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Bedzin?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bedzin.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your mother-- who were the people you lived with? Who's kitchen was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was some friends, my father's friends. They always lived in this part of the city. And they let her stay there in this part of the kitchen. And there were my-- part of my furniture of my room in that part of the kitchen.

speaker: interviewer
text: These friends were Jewish or not Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish, Jewish. Ernst was their name, I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you moved--

speaker: interviewee
text: I came-- I came back to my mother. I moved back to my mother. But I was illegal there. I had no identification papers. I had no coupons for bread. I had no coupons for-- for clothes. I wasn't allowed to go out, because anybody ask me who I am, they-- they put me into jail. I thought the worst thing what can happen is to go to jail. I didn't know about extermination then-- camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you do? You remained hidden in this kitchen?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I remained hidden. Sometime I used to go out with my girlfriends, you know, they hid me. And I was nine weeks with my mother. And one night, after the nine weeks-- knock on the door-- the police. They took me away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they-- they didn't take your mother--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: They took you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They took only me. And they took me into a big-- that-- I remember that it used to be a orphanage, this hole that they took me there. And there, I saw lots of people-- girls about my age, and older women, and men. And straight away was a segregation-- like a girls my-- my-- you know, not married girls, and the married women, and the men till 40, and after 40. And they segregated us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you-- you went with a group of young women?

speaker: interviewee
text: Young women.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you-- and you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: And they took us to the next city, the-- the one-- the-- the-- the young girls and the younger men-- the not married girls and the younger mens-- went to another city, to Sosnowiec again, where we went to-- like, into a camp in between.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. let's stop there.
